title: The Twelve Kālīs and Śāktopāya in the Tantrāloka
subtitle: Vikalpasaṃskāra, guru classification, the 4×3 Kālī system, mantravīrya, SAUḤ and KHPHREṀ

summary.one_line: Abhinavagupta's exposition of the Empowered Means (Śāktopāya) in Āhnika 4 of the Tantrāloka, centred on the Twelve Kālīs of the Krama system.
summary.scope: Covers vikalpasaṃskāra (verses 1-7), guru classification (verses 33-78ab), the Twelve Kālīs (verses 146cd-173ab), mantravīrya (verses 181cd-193), SAUḤ and KHPHREṀ.

sources.label: Tantrāloka of Abhinavagupta, Volume 3 (Āhnika 4), translated by Mark S.G. Dyczkowski

body.text: Now we will speak of the group of Empowered Means which serve to attain the consciousness of the Supreme Self. 'The group of (Empowered) Means' because they are many, beginning with the purification of thought and the rest. He who wishes to enter into this, the Supreme Lord's nature described in the previous chapter, must purify his thought properly and without delay. Practice here centres on perception (jñāna), that involves working with the flux of vikalpa. The purpose of practice in the Empowered Means is to make vikalpa completely clear (sphuṭa), involving a progressive transition from the domain of vikalpa to the domain devoid of vikalpa. (TĀv 4/1-7, pp. 1-10)

He alone is the true teacher wherever the goal, which is knowledge of ultimate reality, is seen to be. Apply the network of means there to attain the knowledge the teacher has attained spontaneously. According to the Brahmayāmala, apart from the knowledge of ultimate reality, there is no other distinguishing characteristic of a true teacher. Four types of teacher are distinguished: the spontaneously enlightened (sāṁsiddhika) or uncreated (akalpita) teacher is the best; the uncreated-cum-created (akalpitakalpaka) teacher has attained realization through practice but may not be fully established; the created-cum-uncreated (kalpitākalpita) and the created (kalpita) teachers are progressively lower. Direct initiation by the Goddess is also possible. (TĀv 4/33-78ab, pp. 43-93)

This same consciousness manifests by virtue of its freedom as both internal and external reality, even as it persists as essentially pure reflective awareness. Consciousness freely assumes two forms — internal and external, subjective and objective. The following exposition, the core of which is the Cycle of the Twelve Kālīs, is an analysis of the reflective awareness of the process which is consciousness taught in the Krama system. The Twelve Kālīs are energies or aspects of Kālasaṁkarṣiṇī, the Goddess of the Kashmiri Kālī Krama, who is experienced by the awareness of their incessant activity. (TĀv 4/122cd-124, pp. 149-154)

The four Kālīs who consume the sphere of objectivity are: 1) Sṛṣṭikālī — the Kālī of Creation, the emanation of objectivity; 2) Raktakālī — the Red Kālī, the persistence of objectivity; 3) Sthitināśakālī — the Kālī of the Destruction of Persistence, the withdrawal of objectivity; 4) Yamakālī — the Kālī of Restriction, the inexplicable state of objectivity. These four phases — emanation, persistence, withdrawal, and the inexplicable fourth state — are repeated across the three domains of objectivity, means of knowledge, and subjectivity, yielding the full set of twelve Kālīs. (TĀv 4/148-151, pp. 199-218)

The four Kālīs who consume the sphere of the means of knowledge are: 5) Saṁhārakālī — the Kālī of Withdrawal, the emergence of the means of knowledge; 6) Mṛtyukālī — the Kālī of Death, the persistence of the means of knowledge; 7) Rudrakālī — the Fierce Kālī, the withdrawal of the means of knowledge; 8) Mārtaṇḍakālī — the Kālī of the Sun, the inexplicable state of the means of knowledge. The Sun (Mārtaṇḍa) represents the means of knowledge, the extroverted form of the subject, and these Kālīs consume the cognitive processes that mediate between subjectivity and objectivity. (TĀv 4/152-163, pp. 218-235)

The four Kālīs who consume the sphere of subjectivity are: 9) Paramārkakālī — the Kālī of the Supreme Sun, the emanation of the subject; 10) Kālāgnirudrakālī — the Kālī of Rudra the Fire of Time, the persistence of the subject; 11) Mahākālakālī — the Kālī of the Great Time, the withdrawal of the subject; 12) Mahābhairavaghoracaṇḍakālī — the Horrific and Fierce Kālī of the Great Bhairava, the inexplicable state of the subject. These twelve Kālīs together constitute the complete cycle of perception, and the yogi who contemplates them as the very activity of his own consciousness attains liberation. (TĀv 4/164-173ab, pp. 235-252)

The universal pulse of the Heart of consciousness is the vitality of Mantra (mantravīrya). The self-awareness from which all things have flowed forth and dissolved away within the Heart of consciousness, present in the beginning and end of perception, is called according to the Spanda teachings the universal vibration of consciousness (sāmānyaspanda), which is the outpouring of consciousness within one's own nature. This vibration is that subtle motion which is the non-different radiance, the wave of the ocean of consciousness. Mantra is effective because it is one with this pulsation of consciousness. (TĀv 4/181cd-186ab, pp. 280-286)

SAUḤ is the supreme Heart of Parā, the root mantra of the Trika goddess. Abhinava reports that there are several variants of this monosyllabic Vidyā, including SHAUḤ and HSAUḤ. The three letters of SAUḤ — S, AU, and the visarga H — encompass the entire spectrum of reality from the first stirring of consciousness to its final emission into objectivity. KHPHREṀ is the seed-syllable of Kālasaṁkarṣiṇī, the goddess of the Krama. 'I' consciousness is the vitality and heart of all mantras. The true repetition of mantra (japya) is not mere mechanical recitation but the absorption of the mind into the pulsation of consciousness that is the mantra's essence. (TĀv 4/186cd-194, pp. 286-299)

The process of emanation begins with Being — sat — denoted by 'S', the first letter of the syllable SAUH. 'Being' is understood here to be the Being of phenomenal existence, which is the sphere of duality — the Egg of Māyā — experienced as rooted in the Being of Brahman, the Absolute. As the diphthong AU emerges in the course of the utterance of SAUḤ, he contemplates how the undefined, inner manifestation of Being emerges into the flow of the energies of the reflective awareness of the Light of consciousness. The three letters of SAUḤ — S, AU, and the visarga H — encompass the entire spectrum of reality from the first stirring of consciousness to its final emission into objectivity. (TĀv 4/186cd-188ab, pp. 286-295)

SAUḤ, the seed-syllable of Parā, the supreme goddess of the Trika, is that of emanation (sṛṣṭibīja), whereas Piṇḍanātha (KHPHREṀ), the seed-syllable of Kālī Kālasaṁkarṣiṇī, the supreme goddess of the Krama, which Abhinavagupta expounds next, is that of withdrawal (saṁhārabīja). Abhinava beautifully integrates in this way the two Tantric schools of his Anuttara Trika. Kālī consciousness taught in the Krama consumes duality. Parā consciousness taught in Trika emits oneness. Initially, that Being is external, and is consumed by the fire of consciousness (R), until all that remains is a residue of inner resonant awareness (PH). Then it becomes the Sky of consciousness (KH) completely, and so, by the field of the three powers (E), it becomes the awareness (M) to then finally dissolve away into the essential nature of withdrawal (saṁhāra) of phenomenal manifestation. (TĀv 4/189cd-191ab, pp. 291-297)

And thus, initially, this Goddess (of cognitive consciousness), Who is pure (and unsullied by any form), (becomes) eager to manifest (objectivity as if it were other than herself) in this way, (and) ideates (and brings about) emanation. For this reason, she is known in the scriptures as Sṛṣṭikālī, the Kālī of Creation. The four Kālīs who consume the sphere of objectivity are: 1) Sṛṣṭikālī — the Kālī of Creation, the emanation of objectivity; 2) Raktakālī — the Red Kālī, the persistence of objectivity; 3) Sthitināśakālī — the Kālī of the Destruction of Persistence, the withdrawal of objectivity; 4) Yamakālī — the Kālī of Restriction, the inexplicable state of objectivity. These four phases — emanation, persistence, withdrawal, and the inexplicable fourth state — are repeated across the three domains of objectivity, means of knowledge, and subjectivity, yielding the full set of twelve Kālīs. (TĀv 4/148-151, pp. 199-218)

Thus, in this way, the group of four goddesses beginning with (the Kālī) of Emanation, who delights in consuming (and assimilating into consciousness) the objective aspect, has been described. Now (he goes on to) describe the group of four goddesses beginning with (the Kālī) of Withdrawal, who are skilled in consuming the (four) aspects of the means of knowledge. The four Kālīs who consume the sphere of the means of knowledge are: 5) Saṁhārakālī — the Kālī of Withdrawal, the emergence of the means of knowledge; 6) Mṛtyukālī — the Kālī of Death, the persistence of the means of knowledge; 7) Rudrakālī — the Fierce Kālī, the withdrawal of the means of knowledge; 8) Mārtaṇḍakālī — the Kālī of the Sun, the inexplicable state of the means of knowledge. The Sun (Mārtaṇḍa) represents the means of knowledge, the extroverted form of the subject. (TĀv 4/152-163, pp. 218-235)

As (consciousness) is reflective awareness (parāmarśa), its spontaneous and perpetually emergent (sadodita) resonance (dhvani) (of pure self-awareness, which is the supreme level of Speech,) is called the supreme, Great Heart (SAUḤ). This Heart is the supreme nectar of Bhairava consciousness. The universal pulse of the Heart of consciousness is the vitality of Mantra (mantravīrya). The self-awareness from which all things have flowed forth and dissolved away within the Heart of consciousness, present in the beginning and end of perception, is called according to the Spanda teachings the universal vibration of consciousness (sāmānyaspanda), which is the outpouring of consciousness within one's own nature. This vibration is that subtle motion which is the non-different radiance, the wave of the ocean of consciousness. Mantra is effective because it is one with this pulsation of consciousness. (TĀv 4/181cd-186ab, pp. 279-286)

gaps: Detailed exposition of the Krama lineages and the appendices on the thirteen Kālīs need integration
Practical aspects of inner worship and the application of mantravīrya in meditation need expansion

issues.title: Krama lineages section not covered
issues.description: The passage on Krama lineages (verses 173cd-181ab) and the appendices on the thirteen Kālīs are not yet extracted.

issues.title: Four Kālīs of subjectivity passage needs more detail
issues.description: The passage on the four Kālīs of subjectivity (Paramārkakālī, Kālāgnirudrakālī, Mahākālakālī, Mahābhairavaghoracaṇḍakālī) is currently a summary. Detailed exposition with verse references would strengthen the RO.

versions.summary: Initial RO created from Tantrāloka Volume 3, Āhnika 4. Covers vikalpasaṃskāra, guru types, the 4×3 Kālī system, mantravīrya, SAUḤ and KHPHREṀ.
Expanded with 5 additional passages: detailed SAUḤ three-letter analysis, KHPHREṀ as saṃhāra-bīja, detailed exposition of the four Kālīs of objectivity and means of knowledge, and mantravīrya.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state from Tantrāloka Volume 3
Expanded with passages on SAUḤ, KHPHREṀ, four Kālīs of objectivity and means of knowledge, and mantravīrya
